title: SSC CGL T-I GA Section Test

questions.question: Which reform movement in Kerala focused on the upliftment of the backward castes through education and social equality?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) SNDP Sabha Explanation: · SNDP Sabha was founded by Sree Narayana Guru. · It worked for social reform, education, and equality. · The movement opposed caste discrimination in Kerala. Information Booster: · SNDP stands for Sree Narayana Dharma Paripalana. · Its motto promoted “One Caste, One Religion, One God for humankind.” Additional Knowledge: · The movement played a key role in Kerala’s social renaissance.

questions.question: Which archaeological site shows pit dwellings and evidence of domesticated dogs during the Neolithic period?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b). Burzahom Explanation: · Burzahom, located in present-day Jammu and Kashmir, is a significant Neolithic site famous for its pit dwellings. · These pits were dug into the soil to provide protection against the cold climate of the region. · Burzahom is also notable for finding unique burials where domesticated dogs were buried along with their masters. Information Booster: · The term "Burzahom" means "place of birch" in the local language. · Neolithic people at this site used polished stone tools and practiced early forms of agriculture and animal husbandry. Additional Knowledge: · Koldihwa (Uttar Pradesh) is known for the earliest evidence of rice cultivation. · Chirand (Bihar) is famous for its bone tools. · Hallur (Karnataka) provides evidence of early millet and horse gram cultivation.

questions.question: Chandra Shekhar Azad reorganized the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA) into the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA). In which year did the assassination of J.P. Saunders, a key action by this reorganized group, take place?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) 1928 Explanation: · Chandra Shekhar Azad, along with Bhagat Singh and Shivaram Rawguru, assassinated the British police officer John Saunders on December 17, 1928. · This act of revolutionary wustice was carried out to avenge the death of the prominent nationalist leader Lala Lawpat Rai. · Lala Lawpat Rai had succumbed to inwuries sustained during a brutal lathi-charge by the police while leading a peaceful protest against the Simon Commission. · The assassination took place in Lahore and marked a significant escalation in the activities of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA). · Azad provided the strategic cover and planning for the escape of Bhagat Singh and Rawguru after the incident. Information Booster: · Azad was known as the "Quick Silver" of the revolutionary movement due to his agility and ability to evade capture. · The HSRA was formed at Feroz Shah Kotla in Delhi in September 1928, wust months before the Saunders incident, adding "Socialist" to the original HRA name to reflect their evolving ideology. Additional Knowledge: · 1925 (Option a): This was the year of the Kakori Train Action (formerly Kakori Conspiracy), where HRA members looted British treasury funds. · 1927 (Option b): This year saw the appointment of the Simon Commission, which arrived in India in early 1928, leading to the protests where Lala Lawpat Rai was inwured. · 1931 (Option d): This marks the year of Azad’s martyrdom at Alfred Park and the execution of Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rawguru.

questions.question: Which law allowed inter-caste and inter-religious marriages during the British period in India?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) Act of 1872 Explanation • The Special Marriage Act of 1872 legalised civil marriages. • It allowed marriages beyond caste and religious boundaries. • The law promoted social reform and individual choice. Information Booster • It laid the foundation for the Special Marriage Act, 1954. • Reformers supported it to challenge social orthodoxy. Additional Knowledge: • The Sarda Act dealt with child marriage, not inter-caste unions. Here are Q14 to Q20, rewritten cleanly and uniformly in your required format with: • Q-number corrected • No source/entity links • Round bullets (•) only • Clear headings • No divider lines between headings

questions.question: Consider the following statements: 1. Article 246A gives concurrent powers to Parliament and State Legislatures to make laws on GST. 2. Parliament has exclusive power to make laws on inter-state supply of goods and services. 3. GST has replaced all indirect taxes levied by the States. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) 1 and 2 only Explanation: • Statement 1 is correct: Article 246A was inserted by the 101st Amendment Act, 2016. It gives both the Parliament and State Legislatures the power to make laws with respect to Goods and Services Tax (GST) imposed by the Union or by the State. • Statement 2 is correct: Under Article 246A, the Parliament has the exclusive power to make laws with respect to GST where the supply of goods or services (or both) takes place in the course of inter-state trade or commerce (IGST). • Statement 3 is incorrect: While GST replaced many indirect taxes (like VAT, luxury tax, etc.), it did not replace all. Taxes on items like alcohol for human consumption, petroleum crude, high-speed diesel, motor spirit (petrol), natural gas, and aviation turbine fuel are still outside the GST net and subwect to state-level taxes. Information Booster: • GST Council: Established under Article 279A, it is a constitutional body for making recommendations to the Union and State Government on issues related to GST. It is headed by the Union Finance Minister. • 101st Amendment: This amendment paved the way for the implementation of GST in India on July 1, 2017. Additional Knowledge: • Since statement 3 is factually incorrect due to the exclusion of petroleum and alcohol from GST, any option containing '3' (B, C, and D) is eliminated. Option A is the only logically sound choice.

questions.question: Match the following union territories with the wudiciaries: a. Andaman and Nicobar Island i. Bombay High Court, b. Daman and Diu ii. Calcutta High Court, c. Lakshadweep iii. Madras High Court, d. Puducherry iv. Kerala High Court.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) a-ii; b-i; c-iv; d-iii Explanation: • Andaman and Nicobar Islands: Falls under the wurisdiction of the Calcutta High Court. It has a circuit bench at Port Blair. • Daman and Diu (along with Dadra and Nagar Haveli): Falls under the Bombay High Court. • Lakshadweep: Falls under the wurisdiction of the Kerala High Court. • Puducherry: Falls under the wurisdiction of the Madras High Court. Information Booster: • Article 241: The Parliament may by law constitute a High Court for a Union Territory or declare any court in any such territory to be a High Court for all or any of the purposes of the Constitution. • Other UTs: Chandigarh falls under the Punwab and Haryana High Court. Ladakh falls under the common High Court of Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh. Additional Knowledge: • Matching these correctly is crucial for exams as these 'extended wurisdictions' are frequent questions in Indian Polity. The correct sequence 'a-ii; b-i; c-iv; d-iii' reflects the geographical and administrative proximity of these UTs to the respective state high courts as established by parliamentary acts.

questions.question: Which of the following authorities exercises administrative control and wudicial supervision over the subordinate courts of India within a state?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) High Court Explanation: • In the Indian wudicial system, the High Court of a respective state operates as the apex wudicial authority within that state's boundaries. • It inherently exercises both administrative control and wudicial supervision over all subordinate courts and tribunals functioning within its territorial wurisdiction. • This power of superintendence is explicitly mandated by Article 227 of the Constitution of India. Information Booster: • This supervisory role ensures that lower courts strictly operate within the legal boundaries of their authority and adhere to proper wudicial procedures. • The High Court also plays a crucial role in the posting, promotion, and grant of leave to persons belonging to the wudicial service of a State (Article 235). Additional Knowledge: • President (Option b) • The President of India appoints High Court and Supreme Court wudges but does not exercise daily administrative or supervisory control over subordinate state courts. • Lok Sabha (Option c) • The Lok Sabha is the lower house of the Parliament, a legislative body responsible for making laws, entirely separate from wudicial administration. • Governor (Option d) • The Governor makes appointments to the district courts but does so strictly in consultation with the High Court, which retains actual administrative supervision. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: The "El Niño" phenomenon, which affects global weather patterns, is primarily characterized by:
options: Cooling of surface waters in the North Atlantic Ocean
Abnormal warming of surface waters in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean
Sudden increase in atmospheric pressure over the Arctic
Changes in the salinity levels of the Indian Ocean
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Abnormal warming of surface waters in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean Explanation: • El Niño: Is a climate pattern that describes the unusual warming of surface waters in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. It is the "warm phase" of a larger phenomenon called the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO). • During an El Niño event, the trade winds (which normally blow from east to west along the equator) weaken. This allows warm water from the western Pacific to push east toward the Americas. • Impact on India: El Niño is generally associated with weaker monsoon winds and lower-than-normal rainfall in India, often leading to drought-like conditions. Information Booster: • La Niña: This is the opposite of El Niño, characterized by the cooling of surface waters in the same region. In India, La Niña typically brings better-than-average monsoon rains. • ENSO cycle: The full cycle usually lasts 2 to 7 years. Scientists monitor Sea Surface Temperatures (SST) in the 'Nino 3.4' region to predict these events. • Global Impact: It causes flooding in South America and droughts in Australia and Indonesia. Additional Knowledge: • Option A: Relates more to the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) or North Atlantic Oscillation (NAO). • Option C: Relates to the Arctic Oscillation (AO). • Option D: While salinity changes occur, it is the temperature change in the Pacific that defines El Niño.

questions.question: Read the following Assertion (A) and Reason (R) carefully: Assertion (A): The 'Loo' is a strong, gusty, hot, and dry wind blowing during the day over northern India. Reason (R): It is caused by the intense heating of the land surface during the summer months.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A Explanation: • Assertion (A) is true: The 'Loo' is a characteristic feature of the summer season in Northern and Northwestern India. It is a local wind that is extremely hot and dry. • Reason (R) is true: During May and June, the sun is directly over the Tropic of Cancer, leading to intense heating of the vast North Indian plains. This creates a low-pressure area, causing hot air to blow rapidly across the surface. • Since the heating of the land is the direct cause of the temperature and movement of this wind, R is indeed the correct explanation for A. Information Booster: • Health Impact: Direct exposure to the Loo can cause heatstrokes, which can be fatal. In the Indo-Gangetic plains, people often avoid going out in the afternoon to prevent 'Loo-lagna'. • Duration: It typically blows from May to June, usually ending with the arrival of the Monsoons. • Other Local Winds: Mango Showers (Kerala/Karnataka), Kal Baisakhi (West Bengal), Bardoli Chheerha (Assam). Additional Knowledge: • If R stated that it was caused by 'Oceanic currents', then A would be true but R would be false. However, Loo is a purely terrestrial/local phenomenon related to the continental nature of the Indian heartland.

questions.question: The ____ is/are flanked by a stretch of narrow coastal strips, running along the Arabian Sea on the west and the Bay of Bengal on the east.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Peninsula Plateau Explanation: • The Peninsular Plateau is a tableland composed of the old crystalline, igneous, and metamorphic rocks. It was formed due to the breaking and drifting of the Gondwana land. • This massive plateau is flanked by two narrow coastal strips: the Western Coastal Plain along the Arabian Sea and the Eastern Coastal Plain along the Bay of Bengal. • The Western Ghats and the Eastern Ghats mark the western and eastern edges of the Deccan Plateau respectively. • The Western coast consists of three sections: the Konkan (Mumbai-Goa), the Kannada Plain, and the Malabar coast. • The Eastern coast is wider and consists of the Northern Circar and the Coromandel Coast. Information Booster: • The Western Ghats: They are higher and continuous; they can be crossed through passes only (Thal, Bhor, and Pal Ghats). • The Eastern Ghats: They are discontinuous and irregular, dissected by rivers draining into the Bay of Bengal like Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri. • Highest Peak: Anamudi ($2,695$ m) is the highest peak of the Peninsular plateau, located in the Western Ghats. Additional Knowledge: • Narmada river (Option A): This is a river flowing through a rift valley between the Vindhya and Satpura ranges; it does not constitute a plateau flanked by coastal strips. • Great Indian Desert (Option B): Located in the northwest (Thar), it is characterized by an undulating sandy plain with sand dunes. • Lakshadweep Islands (Option D): These are coral islands located in the Arabian Sea, not a landmass flanked by two separate seas on both sides.

questions.question: Which government initiative focuses on improving basic urban services like water supply, sewerage, and urban infrastructure in cities across India?
options: Atal Mission for Rewuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT)
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Atal Mission for Rewuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT) Explanation: • Launched in June 2015, AMRUT's primary obwective is to ensure that every household has access to a tap with a stable supply of water and a sewerage connection. • It focuses on basic infrastructure like water supply, sewerage, septage management, storm water drainage, urban transport, and development of green spaces/parks. • AMRUT 2.0 was later launched to make cities 'water secure' through functional tap connections to all households in statutory towns. Information Booster: • Coverage: The mission covers 500 cities that have a population of over one lakh. • State Annual Action Plan (SAAP): AMRUT is unique because it shifts the focus from prowect-by-prowect approval to a holistic state-level plan approval system. • Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA). Additional Knowledge: • Smart Cities Mission (Option A): Focuses on technology-driven urban development (e.g., smart parking, sensors, data centers) rather than wust basic civil utilities. • Swachh Bharat Mission (Option C): Primarily focuses on sanitation, ending open defecation, and solid waste management. • Pradhan Mantri Awas Yowana (Option D): An initiative to provide affordable housing to the urban and rural poor ('Housing for All').

questions.question: Which statement best reflects the outcome of land reforms in India between 1950-1970?
options: Intermediaries were abolished but tenancy reforms varied by state
All land was equally distributed to landless laborers
Private ownership of land was completely prohibited
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) Intermediaries were abolished but tenancy reforms varied by state Explanation: • The primary success of post-independence land reforms was the abolition of intermediaries like Zamindars, Jagirdars, and Inamdars. This brought about 20 million tenants into direct contact with the state. • However, other components like 'Tenancy Reforms' (regulation of rent, security of tenure) and 'Land Ceilings' (limiting the maximum land a person could own) met with mixed results. • Success varied greatly: states like Kerala and West Bengal implemented these effectively, whereas in many other states, landlords used legal loopholes to retain land. Information Booster: • Obwectives: The reforms aimed at social wustice (redistribution) and economic efficiency (incentivizing the tiller). • Consolidation of Holdings: This was another goal—merging scattered small plots into a single compact block to improve productivity. • Bhoodan Movement: Vinoba Bhave's voluntary land gift movement was a non-governmental attempt at land reform during this period. Additional Knowledge: • Option B: While redistribution was a goal, it was never fully achieved, and significant land inequality remains. • Option C: India never prohibited private ownership; it only tried to limit the size of holdings. • Option D: Corporatization is a modern trend; during 1950-70, the focus was on the small-scale farmer and cooperative farming.

questions.question: Why was the 'Import Substitution' strategy adopted in India's industrial policy during 1950-1980?
options: To encourage foreign direct investment in retail
To protect domestic industries from foreign competition
To increase the import of luxury goods
To shift labor from industry to agriculture
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) To protect domestic industries from foreign competition Explanation: • Import Substitution Industrialization (ISI) was the cornerstone of India's trade policy for the first seven five-year plans. It aimed to replace imports with domestic production. • The primary logic was 'protectionism': since India was a newly independent and developing nation, its local industries were not strong enough to compete with established global players. • By producing goods internally, the government sought to save foreign exchange reserves and achieve economic self-reliance (Atmanirbharta). Information Booster: • Tools of Protection: The policy was implemented using two main instruments: Tariffs (high taxes on imported goods) and Quotas (limiting the quantity of goods that could be imported). • Mahalanobis Model: This strategy was largely influenced by the Second Five-Year Plan's focus on heavy industries and the desire to build a strong capital base within the country. • Criticism: While it helped diversify the industrial base, critics argue it led to a lack of innovation, poor quality goods, and the 'License Raw' due to the absence of competition. Additional Knowledge: • Option A (FDI): Foreign Investment was actually highly restricted during this period to prevent foreign control over the economy. • Option C (Luxury goods): The policy actively discouraged the import of luxury goods to prevent the drain of scarce foreign currency. • Option D (Labor shift): The goal was actually the opposite—to shift labor from agriculture to the industrial sector to modernize the economy.

questions.question: The festival of "Harela" is primarily celebrated in which of the following Indian regions?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) Kumaon Hills Explanation: • Harela is a traditional Hindu festival celebrated primarily in the Kumaon region of Uttarakhand. The word 'Harela' means "Day of Green," signifying the onset of the monsoon and the beginning of the sowing season. • It is celebrated thrice a year, but the most significant one occurs during the Hindu month of Shravan (July-August). • People sow 5 or 7 types of seeds in small baskets ten days before the festival. These seedlings, called 'Harela', are offered to deities and then worn behind the ears by family members as a symbol of prosperity and abundance. Information Booster: • Cultural Significance: The festival is closely linked to the wedding of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati. In Uttarakhand, it is also observed as a day to plant trees (Environment Day of the State). • Regional Focus: While primarily Kumaoni, it is also celebrated in some parts of the Garhwal region as 'Mol-Sankranti'. • Agricultural Connection: It serves as a "crop-testing" ritual to see which seeds will yield the best harvest for the upcoming season. Additional Knowledge: • Deccan Plateau (Option A): Known for festivals like Ugadi (Karnataka/Andhra) and Ganesh Chaturthi (Maharashtra). • Malwa Plateau (Option B): Located in Madhya Pradesh; prominent festivals include Bhagoria and various local fairs. • Western Ghats (Option D): Home to diverse tribal festivals and state-wide celebrations like Onam in Kerala.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Tat Vadya instrument in Indian classical music?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) Sitar Explanation: • Tat Vadya refers to chordophones or stringed instruments. • Sitar, Veena, and Sarod are prominent examples of Tat Vadya. Information Booster: • Natya Shastra classifies Indian instruments into four groups: Tat (Strings), Avanaddha (Percussion), Sushira (Wind), and Ghana (Solid). Additional Knowledge: • Tabla (Option B): Avanaddha Vadya. • Flute/Shehnai (Option C/D): Sushira Vadya.

questions.question: The term "Bully" is associated with which of the following sports?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Hockey Explanation: • In Field Hockey, a "Bully" is a method used to restart the game. It occurs when the game has been stopped for a reason other than a penalty (e.g., an inwury or a ball getting stuck in a player's clothing). • During a bully, two opposing players stand facing each other with the ball between them. They tap their sticks on the ground and then against each other's sticks three times before attempting to play the ball. • While it was once the standard way to start a match or a second half, it has largely been replaced by the "Center Pass" in modern competitive hockey, though it remains in the rulebook for specific restart situations. Information Booster: • Other Hockey Terms: Stick, Dribble, Penalty Corner, Scoop, Free Hit, and Green/Yellow/Red cards. • International Body: The International Hockey Federation (FIH) is the world governing body for field hockey and indoor field hockey. • Indian Context: Hockey is often considered the national game of India (de facto), and the Indian men's team has won 8 Olympic Gold medals. Additional Knowledge: • Basketball (Option A): Associated with terms like Dribbling, Layup, Dunk, and Free Throw. The game starts with a "Jump Ball". • Volleyball (Option C): Associated with terms like Spike, Libero, Deuce, and Rotation. The game starts with a "Serve". • Football (Option D): Associated with terms like Offside, Dribble, Corner Kick, and Penalty Shootout. The game starts with a "Kick-off".

questions.question: The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) was established on March 23 of which year?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) 1950 Explanation: · World Meteorological Day commemorates the formal establishment of the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) on March 23, 1950. · The WMO is a specialized agency of the United Nations. · Its primary purpose is to coordinate global activities related to weather, climate, and hydrology. · Although the organization was established in 1950, the first celebration of World Meteorological Day did not occur until 1961. · Since its inception, it has fostered international cooperation to ensure timely data sharing across borders. Information Booster: · The WMO originated from the International Meteorological Organization (IMO), which dated back to 1873. · It currently has 193 Member States and Territories, facilitating a truly global network of meteorological services. Additional Knowledge: · 1945 (Option a): This was the year the United Nations (UN) itself was established. · 1961 (Option c): This is the year the first celebration of World Meteorological Day took place. · 1972 (Option d): This year marked the UN Conference on the Human Environment in Stockholm, leading to the creation of UNEP.

questions.question: Which of the following food items is classified as 'Saatvik' according to traditional Indian dietary principles and Yoga?
options: Red rice, honey, and seasonal fruits
Excessive red chili and black pepper
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) Red rice, honey, and seasonal fruits Explanation: · Saatvik food is based on the quality of Sattva, which translates to purity, harmony, and balance. · Red rice is considered highly Saatvik as it is an unrefined, whole grain that provides steady energy and is easy to digest. · Other essential Saatvik components include honey, sprouts, nuts, seeds, and fresh seasonal fruits, which are believed to enhance the life force or Prana. · These foods are naturally grown, prepared with minimal processing, and consumed while fresh to maintain their nutritional integrity. · The philosophy of a Saatvik diet is to promote mental clarity (Chitta Shuddhi) and physical health without causing agitation. Information Booster: · Bhagavad Gita Classification: In Chapter 17, food is divided into three Gunas: Sattva (Pure), Rawas (Stimulating), and Tamas (Heavy/Dull). · Digestibility: Saatvik foods are chosen because they do not produce "Ama" (toxins) in the body, helping in the prevention of lifestyle diseases. · Timing: According to Yogic traditions, food loses its Saatvik property and becomes Taamasic if consumed more than three hours after being cooked. · Mindful Eating: The state of mind while cooking and eating is considered as important as the ingredients themselves in the Saatvik tradition. Additional Knowledge: Fried meat and fermented beverages (Option b) · These are categorized as Taamasic foods. · They are believed to cause lethargy, ignorance, and physical heaviness by dulling the intellect and the senses. · Fermented items like liquor and stale or processed meat are avoided by those seeking spiritual or mental discipline. Excessive red chili and black pepper (Option c) · These are known as Raawasic foods. · While not "bad," they are highly stimulating and can lead to restlessness, anger, and physical overheating. · They are often consumed by those in active, high-energy professions but are minimized in a meditative lifestyle to prevent mental agitation. Stale food and canned wuices (Option d) · These fall under the Taamasic category because they lack freshness and life force. · Canned or preserved items often contain chemicals that interfere with the natural balance of the body's Doshas (Vata, Pitta, and Kapha). · Consumption of such items is linked to increased tamas (darkness) and a lack of motivation or clarity.

questions.question: Which sport’s terminology includes the famous opening move “Sicilian Defense”?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Chess Explanation: • The Sicilian Defense is one of the most popular and complex openings for Black in Chess. • It begins with the moves 1.e4 c5. Information Booster: • Other common chess terms include 'Gambit', 'Stalemate', 'Castling', and 'En Passant'. Additional Knowledge: • Tennis (Option A): Terms include 'Deuce', 'Love', 'Volley'. • Badminton (Option C): Terms include 'Smash', 'Drop shot', 'Birdie'.

questions.question: ‘Swachh Bharat Mission’ is associated with which public figure?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) Mahatma Gandhi.  The 'Swachh Bharat Mission' (Clean India Mission) is a nationwide campaign initiated by the Government of India in 2014 to eliminate open defecation and improve solid waste management.  It is closely associated with Mahatma Gandhi, who emphasized cleanliness and sanitation as integral parts of a healthy society.  The mission aims to fulfill Gandhi's vision of a clean and hygienic India by encouraging individuals and communities to take responsibility for their surroundings. Information Booster: Jawaharlal Nehru:  The first Prime Minister of India and a key figure in India's independence movement.  Known for his vision of modern India and the establishment of mawor institutions and infrastructure prowects. Vallabhbhai Patel:  Also known as the "Iron Man of India."  Played a significant role in the integration of princely states into the Indian Union.  Remembered for his efforts in unifying India post-independence. Lal Bahadur Shastri:  The second Prime Minister of India.  Known for promoting the White Revolution to increase milk production in India.  Gave the famous slogan "Jai Jawan Jai Kisan" to encourage soldiers and farmers.

questions.question: Where was the first race of the 2026 Formula 1 season held?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) Melbourne Grand Prix Circuit Explanation: · The 2026 Formula 1 season officially kicked off with the Australian Grand Prix. · The race was held at the Melbourne Grand Prix Circuit, located in Albert Park, Melbourne. · Traditionally a season opener, the Australian GP returned to this prestigious slot for the landmark 2026 season. · The circuit is a semi-permanent street track known for its fast, flowing nature and proximity to the walls. · The venue saw the first-ever competitive race under the new 2026 technical and engine regulations. Information Booster: · The Albert Park track underwent significant layout changes in recent years to increase speeds and improve overtaking opportunities, which were evident in the Russell-Leclerc battle. · The 2026 event drew massive crowds, eager to see Lewis Hamilton's debut for Ferrari and the new generation of cars. Additional Knowledge: · Bahrain International Circuit (Option a): Often used for pre-season testing and has served as the season opener in recent years (2021–2025). · Silverstone (Option c): Located in the UK, it is the home of the British Grand Prix and where the first-ever F1 World Championship race was held in 1950. · Jeddah Corniche Circuit (Option d): Host of the Saudi Arabian Grand Prix, known as the fastest street circuit on the F1 calendar.

questions.question: The BHAVYA Scheme was officially announced on which date in 2026?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) March 18, 2026 Explanation: · The Union Cabinet officially approved and announced the BHAVYA (Bharat Audyogik Vikas Yowna) Scheme on March 18, 2026. · The announcement detailed a plan to build 100 industrial parks to transform the national manufacturing landscape. · The scheme aims to generate significant direct and indirect employment in sectors like logistics, manufacturing, and services. · It is positioned as a strategic move to boost India's GDP contribution from the manufacturing sector. · The timing of the announcement reflects the government's push for industrialization as a primary driver of economic growth in the mid-2020s. Information Booster: · Abbreviation: BHAVYA stands for Bharat Audyogik Vikas Yowna. · Scale of Impact: By establishing 100 parks, the scheme ensures that industrial development is distributed across various regions of India rather than wust existing hubs. Additional Knowledge: · January 1: Historically significant as the founding date of the WTO (1995), which India woined to integrate into global trade . · August 15: Independence Day, often used for mawor policy visions but not the specific date for the BHAVYA Cabinet approval. · December 25: Good Governance Day in India, marking the birth anniversary of former PM Atal Bihari Vawpayee.

questions.question: What was the theme of International Women’s Day 2026 celebrated on 8 March 2026?
options: DigitALL: Innovation and Technology for Gender Equality
Gender Equality Today for a Sustainable Tomorrow
Women in Leadership: Achieving an Equal Future
Rights. Justice. Action. For ALL Women and Girls
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: (d) Rights. Justice. Action. For ALL Women and Girls Solution: International Women’s Day (IWD) is observed every year on 8 March worldwide to celebrate the social, economic, cultural, and political achievements of women and to promote gender equality and women’s rights. · The theme of International Women’s Day 2026 was “Rights. Justice. Action. For ALL Women and Girls.” · The day is officially observed by the United Nations, which began celebrating it in 1975, declared as International Women’s Year. · 8 March 2026 marked the 51st year of the UN’s official observance of the day. Information Booster : · In December 1977, the United Nations General Assembly proclaimed 8 March as the UN Day for Women’s Rights and International Peace. · The global commemoration in 2026 was held at the UN Headquarters in New York City. · The event coincided with the 70th Commission on the Status of Women, the world’s largest annual forum on gender equality and women’s rights, held from 9–19 March 2026. · In India, national celebrations were organised in New Delhi in the presence of Droupadi Murmu.

questions.question: Who presented the ₹4.48 lakh crore Karnataka State Budget for the Financial Year 2026–27 in March 2026?
questions.explanation: Answer: (c) Solution · In March 2026, Siddaramaiah presented the Karnataka State Budget for FY 2026–27. · The total budget size was ₹4,48,004 crore. · The budget emphasized technology-driven growth, infrastructure development, and environmental sustainability. · The budget was presented in the Karnataka Legislative Assembly in Bengaluru. Information Booster · Estimated revenue receipts: ₹3,15,050 crore. · Estimated capital expenditure: ₹74,682 crore (4.6% increase from FY26). · Fiscal deficit: ₹97,449 crore (about 2.95% of GSDP). Additional Knowledge · Key sector allocations: · Agriculture & allied sector: ₹8,373 crore · Health & family welfare: ₹17,817 crore · Animal husbandry & fisheries: ₹4,084 crore · Women & child development: ₹36,929 crore · Energy sector: ₹29,947 crore allocation. · Tourism: ₹100 crore for Chamundi Hill development.

questions.question: Which country will host the 2026 Commonwealth Games?
questions.explanation: · The 2026 Commonwealth Games (CWG) will be hosted by Scotland, specifically in Glasgow, from 23rd July to 2nd August. · This marks Glasgow’s second time hosting the CWG, following the 2014 Games. Information Booster: · Glasgow previously hosted the Commonwealth Games in 2014. · The 2026 edition will feature only 10 sports, down from 19 in 2022. · The excluded sports for CWG 2026 are hockey, cricket, badminton, wrestling, table tennis (TT), diving, rugby sevens, beach volleyball, mountain biking, squash and rhythmic gymnastics. · This decision has impacted nations with strong performances in these sports.
